data.title: The Trade Desk (TTD) Stock Hits New 52-Week Low As Tariff Fears Rattle Growth Tech
data.description:
The Trade Desk Inc (NASDAQ:TTD) shares hit a new 52-week low Tuesday as investors fled high-multiple growth names after President Donald Trump threatened a 10% tariff on all goods entering the U.S. from Denmark, Norway, Sweden, France, Germany, the U.K., the Netherlands and Finland starting Feb. 1.

Here’s what investors need to know.

  • Trade Desk stock is testing key support levels. What’s behind TTD weakness?

European Tariffs Put Pressure On Trade Desk's Advertiser Base

The Trade Desk is a pure-play demand-side advertising technology platform whose revenue is tied directly to marketers' digital ad spend. When macro uncertainty rises, global brands typically trim performance and brand campaigns first, pressuring ad-tech platforms like TTD more than diversified software peers.

The targeted countries include many of the consumer, luxury and industrial exporters that rely heavily on U.S. demand and that spend aggressively in programmatic channels such as connected TV and open internet display, core categories on The Trade Desk's Kokai platform.

A 10% tariff on their goods could compress margins and reduce marketing budgets, limiting the volume of biddable impressions flowing through TTD's auction-based system.

That, in turn, would slow the company's take-rate growth just as investors were looking for re-acceleration in international markets. Trump's renewed rhetoric around making Greenland a U.S. territory adds another layer of geopolitical risk, reinforcing the risk-off move in globally exposed ad-tech names.

Recent Earnings Beat, But Analyst Cuts Target On TTD

Fundamentally, The Trade Desk in November posted quarterly results that topped expectations, with revenue of about $739 million, up 18% year over year, and EPS of 45 cents, ahead of consensus, while guiding fourth-quarter revenue to more than $840 million.

However, on Tuesday, BofA Securities analyst Jessica Reif Ehrlich maintained an Underperform rating on The Trade Desk and cut her price target to $40 from $49, amplifying concerns that tariff-driven macro headwinds could cap upside despite solid execution.

Bearish Momentum Signals A Troubling Trend

The stock is currently trading 8.5% below its 20-day simple moving average (SMA) and 10.2% below its 100-day SMA, indicating bearish momentum in the short to medium term. Shares have decreased 72.56% over the past 12 months and are currently positioned closer to their 52-week lows than highs.

The RSI is at a neutral level, suggesting that the stock is neither overbought nor oversold at this time. Meanwhile, MACD is below its signal line, indicating bearish pressure on the stock.

The combination of neutral RSI and bearish MACD suggests mixed momentum.

  • Key Resistance: $41

Earnings Report Insights: What Lies Ahead?

Investors are looking ahead to the next earnings report on Feb. 11.

  • EPS Estimate: 50 cents (Down from 59 cents year-over-year)
  • Revenue Estimate: $841.48 million (Up from $741.01 million YoY)
  • Valuation: P/E of 40.3x (Indicates premium valuation)

Analyst Consensus & Recent Actions: The stock carries a Buy Rating with an average price target of $65.32. Recent analyst moves include:

  • UBS: Buy (Lowered Target to $50 on Jan. 20)
  • B of A Securities: Underperform (Lowered Target to $40 on Jan. 20)
  • Morgan Stanley: Equal-Weight (Lowered Target to $42 on Jan. 13)

Valuation Insight: While the stock trades at a premium P/E multiple, the strong consensus and 15% expected earnings decline suggest analysts view this growth as justification for the 91% upside to analyst targets.

Benzinga Edge Rankings

Below is the Benzinga Edge scorecard for The Trade Desk, highlighting its strengths and weaknesses compared to the broader market:

  • Value: Weak (Score: 48.91/100) — Trading at a steep premium relative to peers.
  • Growth: Strong (Score: 90.67/100) — Indicates robust growth potential.
  • Quality: Moderate (Score: 40.42/100) — Balance sheet remains healthy.
  • Momentum: Bearish (Score: 1.28/100) — Stock is underperforming the broader market.

The Verdict: The Trade Desk’s Benzinga Edge signal reveals a mixed outlook. While the Growth score indicates potential, the low Momentum score warns that the stock is struggling, suggesting investors should approach with caution.

Top ETF Exposure

  • SmartETFs Advertising and Marketing Technology ETF (NYSE:MRAD): 5.15% Weight

Significance: Because The Trade Desk carries significant weight in these funds, any significant inflows or outflows for these ETFs will likely force automatic buying or selling of the stock.

TTD Hits New 52-Week Low

TTD Price Action: The Trade Desk shares closed Tuesday down 3.75% at $34.15, according to Benzinga Pro data.

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: Ahead Of 2026 Tax Season: What Filers Should know
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • The IRS is urging taxpayers to prepare early for the 2026 filing season, citing system updates and process changes.
  • Accessing and verifying an IRS online account is one of the most important steps filers can take before tax season opens.
  • Identity verification through ID.me remains required for many IRS digital services and can take time to complete.
  • Filing early and electronically may help taxpayers avoid delays as the IRS navigates staffing and budget pressures.
  • The agency expects most taxpayers to file electronically, with refund timing tied closely to accuracy and preparation.

The Internal Revenue Service is sending a clear message to taxpayers well ahead of the next filing season: do not wait until the last minute.

In a recent press release titled Prepare to file in 2026, the IRS outlined several steps it says taxpayers should take now to avoid headaches when tax season officially opens. The guidance reflects both routine preparation and a broader reality facing the agency as it heads into 2026 with updated systems, evolving tax rules, and ongoing operational pressure.

While the IRS framed the update as a reminder, the timing suggests something more practical. The agency is encouraging early action at a moment when filing delays, identity verification hurdles, and customer service constraints remain front of mind for millions of households.

Why The IRS Is Pushing Early Preparation

According to the IRS, preparing in advance can make the difference between a smooth filing experience and weeks of frustration.

The agency has emphasized that taxpayers should review prior returns, gather income documents, and confirm personal information before filing begins. The guidance builds on recent IRS messaging that preparing early helps reduce errors that slow processing and refunds.

As CNBC reported, the IRS says preparing early can prevent delays as filing volumes rise and operational capacity remains constrained.

The IRS expects the majority of taxpayers to file electronically again in 2026, continuing a long-running shift toward digital filing. According to the agency, most taxpayers file electronically, a trend that generally leads to faster processing and fewer errors.

The IRS Online Account Is Central To 2026 Filing

One of the most prominent recommendations in the IRS update is for taxpayers to access their IRS online account before tax season opens.

The online account allows filers to view prior tax records, check balances, see notices, make payments, and monitor refund status. It also provides access to certain information returns reported by employers and financial institutions, which can help taxpayers verify income before filing.

According to the IRS, setting up the account early can prevent delays later, particularly if identity verification issues arise close to the filing deadline.

ID.me Remains A Gatekeeper For IRS Services

Accessing many IRS digital tools requires ID.me identity verification, handled by a third-party identity verification provider used across federal agencies.

To complete verification, taxpayers typically need a government-issued ID and either a selfie or a live video call. While the IRS describes the process as a one-time setup, it can take time, especially during peak filing season when demand spikes.

Electronic Filing Is Still The IRS Preference

The agency continues to steer taxpayers toward electronic filing. Historically, electronic returns are processed faster than paper filings, and refund timelines are often shorter when returns are error-free.

Eligible households can also use IRS Free File to submit returns at no cost.

Errors tied to income reporting or tax withholding remain a common source of delays, as do misunderstandings around tax credits.

Refund Timing Depends On Accuracy And Preparation

The IRS cautions that refund processing timelines depend heavily on how accurately returns are prepared.

Returns flagged for errors, identity verification issues, or missing information may take longer to process. The agency also warns about common filing mistakes that frequently slow refunds.

Operational Pressure Is Part Of The Backdrop

The update arrives as scrutiny continues around IRS staffing levels and customer service capacity.

Oversight reports from the Treasury Inspector General for Tax Administration have highlighted challenges tied to staffing reductions and workload pressure during filing seasons.

Coverage from CNN on IRS operations heading into tax season has also underscored how service delays remain sensitive to staffing constraints.

At the same time, Bloomberg has reported on how federal agencies under budget pressure are increasingly shifting responsibility toward taxpayer self-service tools.

What This Means For Taxpayers

For individual filers, the message is simple. Preparation now reduces risk later.

The IRS also reminds taxpayers that help remains available through the Taxpayer Advocate Service for unresolved or systemic issues.

Past reporting from Fox Business on IRS customer service concerns shows that delays peak when millions of filers wait until the same deadlines.

What To Watch Next

The next major milestone is when the 2026 tax filing season opens and the IRS begins accepting returns.

Coverage from Benzinga on retail taxpayers heading into 2026 shows that even small procedural changes can materially affect refund timing and filing strategy.

For now, the IRS message is clear. Act early, verify access, and reduce the chances of costly delays.

Featured Image credit: Author

Benzinga Disclaimer: This article is from an unpaid external contributor. It does not represent Benzinga’s reporting and has not been edited for content or accuracy.

data.title: Valneva Steps Back From US Approval Bid For Chikungunya Vaccine
data.description:
Valneva SE (NASDAQ:VALN) on Monday said it has decided to voluntarily withdraw the biologics license application (BLA) and Investigational New Drug (IND) application for its chikungunya vaccine, Ixchiq, in the U.S.

In August 2025, the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) suspended the license for Ixchiq, citing four new reports of serious adverse events (SAEs) consistent with chikungunya-like illness.

The company had been awaiting further information regarding its formal response to the vaccine license suspension. Valneva was recently informed that the FDA has now placed the Investigational New Drug (IND) on clinical hold pending an investigation of a newly reported foreign Serious Adverse Event (SAE).

There are currently no clinical studies involving Ixchiq that are actively vaccinating participants, and the company intends to move forward with its planned post-marketing clinical activities, subject to further discussions with relevant regulatory authorities.

The SAE occurred outside of the U.S. and involved a younger adult who received three concomitant vaccines, including Ixchiq. Based on the information made available to Valneva, which the company submitted to the U.S. Vaccine Adverse Event Reporting System (VAERS) as well as to all other pharmacovigilance systems in accordance with the product’s license, the case may be plausibly related to Ixchiq vaccination, but causality has not been determined.

The company is actively seeking additional information to further characterize the case.

Valneva continues to engage proactively with health authorities in all territories where Ixchiq is licensed, including Europe, Canada, the United Kingdom, and Brazil.

While Ixchiq is currently focused on travelers to regions where the virus is endemic, such as tropical and subtropical areas in Asia, Africa, and the Americas, and persons for whom vaccination is medically justified based on risk in accordance with the approved label and vaccination guidance, the company on Monday said that Ixchiq’s benefit-risk profile also remains favorable for people living in the endemic and outbreak settings, where Ixchiq may be uniquely positioned as a highly durable single-shot vaccine.

In November 2025, Valneva shared final immunogenicity and safety data on Wednesday from the Phase 2 study, VLA15-221, of the Lyme disease vaccine candidate, VLA15.

Subject to positive Phase 3 data, the partner, Pfizer Inc. (NYSE:PFE), aims to submit a Biologics License Application to the U.S. Food and Drug Administration and a Marketing Authorization Application to the European Medicines Agency in 2026.

Price Action: VALN stock is down 4.37% at $9.63 at the last check on Tuesday.

Photo: Gorodenkoff/Shutterstock
